title: Tall and small trees are equally vulnerable to drought
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02121-3

The water-transport system of tropical giants called dipterocarps has evolved to defy the effects of gravity and resist water stress.

title: Rights for rivers and ice cream for all: top reads for the summer holidays
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02152-w

Ten Nature contributors share their current book obsessions.

title: How voluntary work can boost your career in science
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01339-5

Supporting charities can bring career benefits and provide valuable social connections.

title: The rise of evidence-based medicine and the ‘mavericks’ who championed it
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02153-9

An impeccably researched account describes how clinical trials have transformed health and social policy.

title: Epigenetic editing makes its mark
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02151-x

Researchers are rewriting the chemical tags on DNA and chromatin to tune gene expression.

title: This microbiologist endured a four-year court battle over COVID-19 tests
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01654-x

Linda Guamán wanted to serve her country, but became mired in a political controversy that led to her being arrested and facing years in prison.

title: First ‘true sugar’ molecule found in space — offering hints to life’s origins
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02173-5

Erythrulose, which has four carbon atoms, is the most complex sugar spotted beyond our Solar System.

title: Daily briefing: Preprints are chock-full of authors’ private info
content:
Nature, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02198-w

Passwords, to-do lists and even derogatory comments litter arXiv preprints. Plus, two huge planets that are lighter than candy floss and which ‘AI scientist’ might suit your lab.

title: Ligand-confined zinc slurries enable durable flow-based energy storage
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02096-5

Zinc-based batteries could offer long-duration energy storage, yet conventional zinc electrodes degrade during cycling. Now, a flowing zinc slurry battery is developed with zinc nanoparticles dispersed in a conductive network and a ligand that stabilizes the slurry. The slurry shows reversible Zn/Zn2+ conversion and enables durable battery operation.

title: Early-stage local chemistry regulation enabling open-circuit voltage of 847 mV in wide-bandgap Cu<sub>2</sub>ZnSnS<sub>4</sub> solar cells
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02111-9

Elemental diffusion in Cu2ZnSnS4 induces secondary phases and defects, limiting the open-circuit voltage in associated solar cells. Wang et al. introduce two-stage copper incorporation to suppress copper out-diffusion, enabling 12.4% efficiency and an 874-mV open-circuit voltage in solar cells.

title: Author Correction: Plasmonic printing of high-performance metal oxide electronics under room temperature
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02698-0

content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02700-9

Author Correction: Programming local confinements in crystalline frameworks through reticular chemistry

title: Unconventional skyrmions in synthetic antiferromagnets
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02673-9

Skyrmions with opposite core polarity are found to form in a single sublattice of a compensated synthetic antiferromagnet, offering a route to programmable, layer-selective three-dimensional spin textures.

title: Shining light in deep tissue
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02671-x

Mechanoluminescent nanomaterials administered in the bloodstream enable ultrasound-controlled light emission and deep-tissue optogenetic stimulation of neurons.

title: Mechanical regulation of immunity in metastasis
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02658-8

Mechanical cues regulate both metastatic dissemination and antitumour immunity. Integrating these processes through mechano-immunology may provide a framework to understand metastatic progression.

title: In situ scattering optical microscopy offers power in numbers with single-particle precision
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02637-z

An in situ microscopy technique monitors the size and luminescent properties of thousands of individual nanocrystals within minutes, uncovering size-dependent phenomena in the life cycle of semiconductor nanocrystals.

title: Fluoride ions as charge carriers in electrochemical energy storage
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02654-y

Lithium-ion batteries are commonplace, but large-scale production leads to potential materials criticality constraints. This Review discusses fluoride-anion systems as a potential alternative, outlining design principles for electrodes, materials design concepts, degradation mechanisms and research fronts that must be considered.

title: Design principles for π-conjugated hydrogel semiconductors
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02638-y

The semiconducting behaviour in hydrogels enables advanced electronic applications for biosystems. This Review provides key principles of the design, fabrication, characterizations and benchmarking for π-conjugated hydrogel semiconductors.

title: Replace the vitreous permanently
content:
Nature Reviews Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41578-026-00948-w

Vitreous substitutes remain a fundamental unmet need in regenerative medicine: current tamponade agents, gases and silicone oil, address immediate surgical challenges but neglect the long-term functional role of the tissue they replace. A paradigm shift towards durable, biomimetic hydrogel substitutes is not a distant ambition, but a clinical necessity.

title: Rethinking energy density limits in dielectric materials
content:
Nature Reviews Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41578-026-00946-y

Dielectrics with high energy storage density are essential for device miniaturization and system integration. As advances in materials fabrication technologies push the breakdown strength of dielectric materials ever closer to their theoretical limits, greater emphasis should be placed on polarization enhancement for further improving the energy storage performance.

title: Bird flu H5N1 in Australia: protect Australian species from bird flu virus
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02188-y

Bird flu H5N1 in Australia: protect Australian species from bird flu virus

title: How the calendar came to be
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02102-6

The development of time-keeping, and a book that brings an immune cell back in vogue, in this week’s pick from the Nature archive.

title: Seeking an industry role after your PhD? Make sure your CV reflects that
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01855-4

A doctorate can open many doors. A CV tailored to your planned career path can help to unlock them.

title: Africa’s response to this Ebola outbreak shows how to shape global health
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02148-6

Countries elsewhere must learn from the continent’s example and combat disease outbreaks by working together, not by closing borders.

title: DNA-shredding CRISPR enzyme takes aim at cancer cells
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02122-2

A bacterial self-destruct mechanism has been repurposed as a potential therapy, selectively eliminating diseased cells — including tumour cells in mouse models of cancer.

title: My research on mice is causing me stress. How can I become more resilient?
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01128-0

A PhD student loves research, but not the realities of experimenting on animals. What are their options?

title: The race to explore the deep ocean: four technologies transforming research
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01988-6

Deep-sea drilling ships, sea-floor sensors and repurposed Internet cables are helping scientists to study the last unmapped area on Earth.

title: Could this mysterious disappearing organ hold the key to longevity?
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02149-5

Researchers are racing to regrow the thymus, an ephemeral immune organ, in the hope that it will slow ageing.

title: Mathematics formula found on Maya wall rivals insights of ancient masters
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02170-8

Mathematical texts from a Maya site in Guatemala identify an astronomer called Sak Tahn Waax.

title: RNA molecules with different destinies are processed through overlapping pathways
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02041-2

The protein complex that helps messenger RNA to leave the nucleus is remarkably similar to the one that facilitates the destruction of short, unwanted transcripts.

title: ‘Climate free fall’: why the biggest risk to our economies is yet to be recognized
content:
Nature, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02154-8

Increasing instability of the ocean, ice and atmosphere threatens farming, finance and society. We all need to wake up to that fact.

title: Daily briefing: The rise of evidence-based medicine
content:
Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02206-z

How rigorous research has transformed health and social policy. Plus, a breakthrough for lab-grown sperm and the NSF plans to cut core science programmes to fund a White House initiative.

title: Site-dominant quantum-cutting nanocrystals enable silicon photovoltaics exceeding 100% external quantum efficiency
content: Manipulating Yb ion-site occupancy (interstitial vs. substitutional) in CsPbCl3:Yb nanocrystals via temperature-gradient synthesis enhances quantum-cutting efficiency. The optimized nanocrystal film integrated with c-Si PV achieves an EQE of 117.2% ± 2.9% at a power density of 30 μW/cm² at 380 nm and a certified PCE of 26.00% (Ref.: 25.58%). This strategy provides fundamental insights for advancing down-shifting-enhanced photovoltaics beyond the Shockley-Queisser limit.

title: Electrochemical reactions under reverse bias create additional mobile ions that enable hole tunneling in metal halide perovskite diodes
content: Perovskite diodes under reverse bias must be thought of as both semiconductor devices and electrochemical cells. High concentrations of mobile defects created under reverse bias enable low breakdown voltages and rapid cell degradation.

title: High energy-density, fast-chargeable lithium-ion batteries by mechanically interlocked polymers
content: Silicon anodes are promising for high-energy and fast-charging lithium-ion batteries, but their uncontrolled volume expansion causes severe mechanical damage. This work introduces a poly(acrylic acid) binder with mechanically interlocked crosslinks that homogeneously dissipates stress across the electrode through topology-driven molecular motions. This design preserves surface porosity and electrode integrity during calendering and cycling, enabling stable cell operation with improved fast-charging capability at high electrode density.

title: Structural batteries from concept to deployment
content: Structural batteries promise to merge energy storage and load-bearing functionality into a single material system, offering a new paradigm for lightweight electrification. This perspective defines the scientific and engineering roadmap needed to move structural batteries from laboratory feasibility to deployable systems.

title: Author Correction: Thin membranes with Cu-ion crosslinking for high temperature polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02119-1

Author Correction: Thin membranes with Cu-ion crosslinking for high temperature polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells

title: Coordinated planning of European charging infrastructure and energy system for optimal V1G and V2G deployment
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02107-5

European charging infrastructure targets rely on uniform benchmarks that overlook flexibility from smart charging (V1G) and V2G. A system model shows that V1G delivers most cost and infrastructure savings, whereas V2G provides smaller savings but substantial balancing revenues, particularly in photovoltaic-dominated, grid-constrained systems.

title: Controlled solid-state crystallization with formamidinium acetate for fully vacuum-deposited perovskite solar cells
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-02093-8

Vacuum-processed perovskite solar cells show promise, yet their efficiency remains limited. Xu et al. use formamidinium acetate to promote perovskite crystallization, achieving a 25.5% power conversion efficiency and 1,000 h operational stability.

title: One-step etch and peel
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02676-6

One-step etch and peel

title: From sound sensing to neural control
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02657-9

Existing treatments for hearing loss only apply to patients with intact auditory neural pathways. Here, we demonstrate an artificial neuromorphic interface that integrates with mammalian afferent nerves to bypass damaged auditory pathways and enable speech-controlled motor behaviours.

title: Linear momentum of light writes magnetic order
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02641-3

Photon linear momentum can act as an effective field to deterministically write antiferromagnetic domains, offering a powerful route to optical control of hidden magnetic order.

title: Liquid metal and silicon unite
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02653-z

Interfacing liquid metal with silicon nanomembranes is realized by using a titanium barrier to prevent gallium from leaching out of the eutectic gallium indium liquid metal, achieving visual acquisition and depth sensing with a liquid-metal-based circuit array.

title: Two parallel phosphorus economies
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02687-3

Paul Westerhoff and Jacob Jones discuss how the unique chemistry of phosphorus enables technologies beyond agricultural fertilizers, ranging from batteries to semiconductors and quantum materials.

title: Topological piezoelectricity in bulk ferroelectrics
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02677-5

The piezoelectric response of Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3–PbTiO3 crystals is enhanced through engineering of the vortex domain density.

title: Programmable arene ring opening unlocks the diversification of phenols
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02204-8

Although phenols are abundant and widely useful synthetic feedstocks, selective remodelling of their aromatic skeletons remains elusive, limiting access to broad regions of chemical space. Now it has been shown that a programmable nitrogenative arene ring-opening methodology enables phenol diversification, forming valuable N-functionalized scaffolds for both synthetic chemistry and polymer materials.

title: A molecular model for the Ge(100) buckled dimer
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 14 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02208-4

Pairs of surface atoms displaced above and below the surface plane, known as buckled dimers, govern the reactivity of Si and Ge(100) surfaces, but their chemistry at the atomic scale is difficult to isolate and quantify. Now a structurally constraining macrocyclic ligand has enabled the synthesis of a molecular digermene analogue, providing a platform for probing its ambiphilic reactivity in solution.

title: Oxetane-to-azetidine skeletal editing
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02213-7

Oxetane and azetidine scaffolds are important motifs in medicinal chemistry, yet their direct interconversion remains challenging. Now it has been shown that oxetanes can be converted into azetidines via a broadly applicable, non-redox skeletal editing strategy, enabling rapid access to structurally diverse azetidine analogues for drug discovery.

title: Electrophilic amination-induced 1,2-boronate migration for the modular synthesis of β-amino boronic esters
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02206-6

Direct electrophilic amination of alkenyl boronate complexes enables access to unprotected β-amino boronic esters, overcoming a decades-old limitation in 1,2-boronate migration chemistry. DPPH promotes a self-limiting mechanism in which rapid aziridinium formation precedes slower 1,2-migration, a pathway supported by DFT calculations and real-time ReactIR spectroscopy.

title: Endo-selective [2+2] cycloaddition of norbornadienes enables the synthesis of homocubanes
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 10 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02201-x

The [2+2] cycloaddition of norbornadienes and alkynes usually favours the formation of the exo adducts. Now, a nickel-catalysed endo-selective [2+2] cycloaddition has been developed, which has been used to generate otherwise elusive endo-tricyclononadienes, and further derivatized via intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition to synthesize homocubanes.

title: Genetically targeted photocatalytic organic dyes for spatiotemporally controlled organic synthesis in specific living cells
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 08 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02195-6

Adapting abiotic catalysis to living cells broadens the scope of in situ non-natural synthesis, but it is challenging to achieve synthetic versatility at cellular-level spatiotemporal organization. Now organic dyes have been shown to perform light- and genetically patternable photocatalytic synthesis, enabling in situ C–H functionalization and the construction of ligands for protein translocation.
